modifications.beginner: L-shaped handstand at wall (legs horizontal on wall, hands on floor) as preparation
modifications.fear of inversion: Build through Viparita Karani → Sarvangasana → Sirsasana → Pincha first
modifications.wrist sensitivity: Use parallel bars or yoga blocks under palms to reduce wrist extension

therapeuticUses: Hypertension (carotid sinus pressure reflex — reduces BP)
instructions: Sit in Siddhasana or Padmasana with spine erect.
Inhale completely. Retain the breath (antar kumbhaka).
Drop the chin toward the sternum — into the jugular notch.
Shoulders remain relaxed; do not hunch. Back of neck lengthens.
Hold the lock comfortably for 5–15 seconds.
Release the lock first, then slowly exhale through the nose.
Important: release Jalandhara before releasing Mula Bandha (if combined).
Practise 5 rounds; build duration gradually over weeks.

endocrine: Compression of carotid sinus reduces sympathetic activity — lowers heart rate and blood pressure
Direct thyroid gland compression followed by release increases local circulation — stimulates thyroid in hypothyroidism
neurological: Activates vagus nerve via carotid sinus baroreceptors — powerful parasympathetic induction
respiratory: Trains breath retention capacity; improves CO2 tolerance

modifications.cervical stiffness: Bring chin toward chest without full lock; partial chin tuck only
modifications.beginners: Practise without breath retention initially; just chin-to-chest stretch
modifications.hypothyroid protocol: 3 × 10-second holds after full inhalation, twice daily

instructions: Master Mula Bandha, Uddiyana Bandha and Jalandhara Bandha individually before attempting Maha Bandha.
Sit in Siddhasana or Padmasana. Exhale completely.
Apply Jalandhara Bandha (chin to chest) first.
Apply Uddiyana Bandha (abdominal retraction upward) second.
Apply Mula Bandha (perineal/pelvic floor lock) third.
Hold all three simultaneously on external retention (bahir kumbhaka) for 5–15 seconds.
Release in reverse order: Mula → Uddiyana → Jalandhara.
Inhale slowly; rest 3–5 breaths before next round.

endocrine: Simultaneous stimulation of pituitary (via Jalandhara), adrenal/solar plexus (via Uddiyana) and gonadal/pelvic (via Mula) — comprehensive endocrine activation
pranic: Prevents prana from dissipating upward or downward — maximises pranayamic benefit
cardiovascular: Powerful vagal stimulation reduces resting heart rate
digestive: Combined visceral massage stimulates entire digestive tract

relative: Menstruation (avoid Uddiyana Bandha; Mula and Jalandhara may be performed gently)

modifications.sequence: Never attempt Maha Bandha without proficiency in each individual bandha for minimum 4 weeks
modifications.supervision: Best learnt under direct teacher supervision — not self-taught initially
modifications.health condition: Cardiac and hypertension patients must have medical clearance before practice

instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, hands on thighs.
Exhale completely and retain breath outside (bahir kumbhaka).
Rapidly pump the abdominal wall — in and out — using abdominal muscles only.
Aim for 20–30 pumps per retention. Keep lower back relaxed.
When the urge to breathe becomes strong, relax the abdomen and inhale slowly.
Rest 3–5 breaths between rounds; practise 3–5 rounds.
Best practised on empty stomach — ideally morning, fasted.
Do NOT confuse with Kapalbhati (breath-based) — Agni Sara is purely abdominal without breath movement.

digestive: Direct mechanical massage of all abdominal organs — liver, pancreas, intestines
Activates digestive fire (Agni) — increases enzyme secretion
Most effective naturopathic treatment for atonic constipation
endocrine: Pancreatic stimulation improves insulin sensitivity
Adrenal stimulation via solar plexus activation
metabolic: Increases abdominal heat and metabolic rate — promotes fat burning in abdominal region
detoxification: Lymphatic pumping of mesenteric lymph nodes — enhances gut immunity

relative: Cardiac disease (perform gently; no strong retention)
Diarrhoea or colitis active (avoid until resolved)

modifications.beginners: Practise in sitting with less force; 10 pumps per round, build gradually
modifications.obesity: Standing version as described is easiest; 2 rounds, 20 pumps to start
modifications.DM protocol: 3 rounds × 25 pumps before breakfast — combine with Mandukasana for synergistic effect

therapeuticUses: COPD and asthma (Stage II — strengthens respiratory muscles)
instructions: Viloma Stage I (Interrupted Inhalation): Inhale for 2 seconds, pause 2 seconds (hold without strain), inhale 2 sec, pause 2 sec — continue until lungs are full. Then exhale smoothly.
Viloma Stage II (Interrupted Exhalation): Inhale fully. Exhale 2 seconds, pause 2 seconds, exhale 2 sec, pause 2 sec — continue until lungs are empty. Then inhale smoothly.
Each stage performed for 5 minutes; then switch to the other stage.
No forceful kumbhaka — pauses are gentle, not forceful breath holds.
Sit in Sukhasana or Siddhasana throughout; spine erect.
Regarded as one of the few pranayamas safe in pregnancy (Stage II).

respiratory: Strengthens diaphragm and intercostal muscles incrementally
Stage I increases tidal volume; Stage II improves expiratory reserve volume — beneficial in COPD
cardiovascular: Interrupted exhalation (Stage II) activates parasympathetic system — reduces BP and heart rate
neurological: One of the least stimulating pranayamas — suitable for anxious and hypersensitive patients
obstetric: Recommended in Iyengar yoga tradition as safe pranayama throughout pregnancy — improves foetal oxygenation

absolute: Avoid forceful pauses in cardiac arrhythmia
relative: Active asthma attack (wait until attack resolves)

modifications.pregnancy: Stage II preferred; practise in Savasana or supported Supta Baddha Konasana if sitting uncomfortable
modifications.COPD: Begin with 1-second intervals; prolong to 2–3 sec as capacity builds over weeks
modifications.hypertension: Stage II (interrupted exhalation) is primary — longer exhalation pauses amplify the vagal effect

therapeuticUses: Depression (specific — elevates mood by altering CO2 balance transiently)
instructions: Sit in Sukhasana or Padmasana. Eyes closed. Apply Jalandhara Bandha gently.
Inhale slowly and deeply through both nostrils.
Retain breath (antar kumbhaka) with gentle Jalandhara Bandha; tilt head slightly back.
Fix attention at Ajna chakra (between eyebrows) or Sahasrara (crown).
Hold comfortably for 5–15 seconds — you may experience a swooning or floating sensation.
Lower the head; release Jalandhara. Exhale very slowly through nostrils.
Sit quietly for 3–5 breaths before next round. Maximum 5 rounds.
Practise ONLY under experienced teacher supervision. Never practise alone initially.

neurological: Transient CO2 shift with upward drishti induces altered state — not true fainting but deep meditative absorption
psychological: Documented mood-elevating effect — used in specific depressive states in classical yoga texts
endocrine: Sustained antar kumbhaka with Jalandhara increases intracranial pressure transiently — stimulates pineal and pituitary glands

relative: Anxiety disorders (use only under supervision — can initially worsen anxiety)
Beginners without pranayama foundation of minimum 6 months
contraindications.clinical_note: This pranayama is classified as advanced and potentially hazardous. BNYS doctors should understand it theoretically and refer only to experienced yoga therapists for clinical application. Document in patient records if prescribed.

modifications.safe_alternative: If Moorcha is contraindicated, substitute with Bhramari + Shanmukhi Mudra for similar calming effect
modifications.supervision: Must be taught in person; never via online instruction without physical presence
modifications.beginner_equivalent: Dirgha Pranayama with extended exhalation achieves partial benefit without risk
